Summary: The bloodless capture of the county's seat of power, resolved with a single punch
Key Events: Sergeant Crake defects at Millbrook with five soldiers, warning that Ser Dagric Vane won't fold with the rest of the garrison and will demand a formal duel. At the gate, Vane offers single combat against Brandon's champion; Brandon sends Grondulf. Vane lands one picture-perfect tourney punch to Grondulf's stomach — and Grondulf, entirely unmoved, simply picks him up and sets him down two feet away. "Off his feet," by the letter of the very rules Vane set, ends the fight in seconds. Vane yields and swears fealty on the spot, retiring the phrase "it relocated me, like furniture" into permanent county legend. Inside the fort, Baron Wexley is found hiding, and Reeve Ossric Fenn is caught mid-tiptoe up the keep stairs with a stolen strongbox
Significance: Established the Undergrowth's signature approach — overwhelming force offered as a formality, negotiation doing the actual work — and gave the faction its permanent base, later renamed the Dreadhold
Key Quotes:
- "WAS THAT THE EXCHANGE"
- "Second at the Grand Melee. It relocated me. Like furniture."
- "Ser Dagric Vane yields, and swears his blade to the Dread Lord of the Undergrowth, before these witnesses, may the ballads be kind and the cellars generous."

42.content:
Name: The Grand County Council (event)
Summary: A single-day assembly of all eleven villages in the county at the Dreadhold, run as equal parts grievance court, tax audit, and — per Brandon's own idea — a "controlled honesty experiment"
Key Events: On Clarissa's read that alcohol plus Brandon's truth-compulsion would turn the hall into "a confession booth with better snacks," the county is served wine mixed with local cider (no citrus available for three hundred miles). Village by village, grievances get resolved on the spot: Thornfield (a blacksmith village) trades an unfair charcoal tax for a bulk ore contract and a militia-outfitting deal; Willowmere gets labor help for a flooded meadow the Wexleys never fixed; Ashwick is freed from a timber monopoly run by Fenn's own cousin ("Cut him out. Please."); Fenhollow, represented by Edda, asks only to be remembered on the winter supply list. The day's emotional low point comes when Mosshaven is called and no one stands — the village had been abandoned since autumn, its well poisoned by the (then-unidentified) miasma
Significance: Cemented the Undergrowth as a real government rather than a gang with a fort, and directly seeded the Gray Zone/Drinker expedition via Mosshaven's plight
Key Quotes:
- "You're not asking me to appraise a flower, you're running a controlled honesty experiment on eleven villages using cheap wine and whatever citrus we don't have." — Clarissa
- "That's not a grievance. That's twelve families waiting to hear if they have a home to go back to." — Nessa
- "Neighbors. Eleven villages, one hall, and not a single one of you walked here at spearpoint." — Archlector Melvus

43.content:
Name: The Golden Thread — Brandon and Yminia (ongoing relationship event)
Summary: An unexpected, increasingly mutual, and very embarrassing-for-everyone-involved intimate connection developing between Brandon and Yminia herself, discovered through the same divine thread that delivers her prophecies and dream-visits
Key Events: A dream-audience request for "one little kiss" triggers an intensely reciprocal reaction, since the thread is a literal extension of Yminia's own senses. Pemberly, forced to explain the mechanics under professional obligation, confirmed the thread is bidirectional and carries full sensory fidelity — including images — directly into Yminia's consciousness with no filter and no warning. Brandon tested this, apparently on purpose, and Yminia sent back an "accidental" image clearly cropped and composed on purpose (a bare shoulder, a slipping robe, framed as though caught candidly beside her loom-side bookshelf), then immediately panicked and tried to walk it back as an accident ("THAT WAS THE BOOKSHELF. THE SHOULDER WAS INCIDENTAL. DELETE IT."). Pemberly was forced to open a brand-new file category — RECIPROCAL — for the first time in two hundred years of service. Sir Frederick caught the aftermath of the original incident through the bond and excused himself to breakfast, seeing nothing. Brandon covered the fallout at breakfast with a technically-true "vision from the goddess" story, drawing a correctly-suspicious, wrongly-aimed "WHO?" from Clarissa
Status: Ongoing and unresolved. A single fresh chamomile blossom, left with no note, appeared on Brandon's windowsill the same night
Significance: A major, still-developing relationship thread entirely separate from anything involving Clarissa — her suspicion is a red herring even she doesn't realize is one
Key Quotes:
- "…oh. oh, that's what that feels like from this side. I didn't — the thread is me, it's literally ME—" — Yminia
- "The thread is BIDIRECTIONAL… she doesn't just receive through it. She can send. She has simply chosen not to. So far." — Pemberly
- "THAT WAS THE BOOKSHELF. I WAS SENDING YOU THE BOOKSHELF. THE SHOULDER WAS INCIDENTAL. DELETE IT." — Yminia
- "I AM AWAKE AND THE AETHER LOGS ARE SHOWING A DIVINE RECIPROCAL CONTACT EVENT OF A NATURE I HAVE NO FORM FOR." — Pemberly

44.content:
Name: The Dreadhold, formerly Fort Bramblehold
Summary: The Undergrowth's seat of power. Originally Fort Bramblehold, seat of House Wexley, captured without bloodshed when Grondulf beat Ser Dagric Vane in single combat. Renamed within a day of capture
Features: A curtain wall about twenty feet high, an old-style gatehouse, a keep with a great hall seating forty, a wine cellar Vane guards jealously, a one-armed marble statue of a Wexley ancestor (secretly hollow — the Baron hid in its pedestal during the "siege"), a sour well Grondulf cleared of a dead badger ("CODE VIOLATION SIX. RESOLVED"), and a growing Records office run by Tobbin
Current State: A functioning county government headquarters. Home to the sprout banner (black wool, pale sprout), Marta's tailoring operation, Clarissa's three-page organizational chart, and — as of recently — chamomile flowers growing directly out of the upstairs hallway floor, for reasons everyone is very deliberately not asking about
Key Quotes:
- "Welcome to Bramblehold, Dread Lord. The suite with the south windows is mine. Everything else, I believe, is yours." — Baron Wexley, on surrender
- "Bramblehold because the Dreadhold, when a podunk region grew into a potential political powerhouse overnight." — Brandon

45.content:
Name: Aurelium
Summary: Capital city of Flameworth, renamed after King Aurelian roughly thirty years ago. Seat of the crown, the Chancellery, and the Feast of Saint Halvard
Features: White walls, gold rooftops, banners on every tower. Roughly half a million people. Four gates, a river quarter, tourney grounds. A summoning chamber beneath the palace, past three locked doors, with a fresh chalk circle sitting atop dozens of older scorch rings — far more than the four "official" Chosen the crown admits to
Notable Residents/Ties: King Aurelian, the (unnamed) Lord Chancellor, Dowager Baroness Ophelia Wexley (townhouse on Goldrow), Master Quillon the tailor (also Goldrow), Master Halloway's Guild superiors
Status: The Undergrowth's destination for the Feast of Saint Halvard, and the presumed base of the suspected Chancellery conspiracy against Brandon
Key Quotes:
- "From the hills it's gorgeous. White walls, gold rooftops, banners on every tower like the whole city is dressed for a party that never ends. Then you get inside and it's half a million people stacked on top of each other." — Clarissa

46.content:
Name: The Mire (also called the Gray Zone)
Summary: A miasma-corrupted marsh south of the county — the source of thirty years of disappearances, and the body the Drinker wore
Detection Methods: Greenmoss carried by every traveler in an open pouch (stays green in clean air, goes white in bad aether, crumbles to powder in true miasma); Gerald's runes (dim as corruption thickens, go fully dark in the worst of it — "he went dark once outside the Weeping Tower"); and Button's nose (the deeper in, the more it smells like honey — "if it smells like honey, you are already standing in it")
Status: Cleared. The Drinker was mercy-killed and transformed into a seed, later planted at Mosshaven. The mire itself is presumably reverting to ordinary marshland
Key Quotes:
- "Dawn-forged steel reacts to corrupted aether — the runes dim as the concentration thickens. Full glow means clean air. Half glow means caution." — Vane
- "THE SWEET WATER SMELLS LIKE SOMETHING PRETENDING TO BE FLOWERS. THE DEEPER IN, THE SWEETER IT GETS. IF IT SMELLS LIKE HONEY, YOU ARE ALREADY STANDING IN IT." — Button

47.content:
Name: Millbrook
Summary: A market town in the county, site of Reeve Fenn's public exposure and Sergeant Crake's defection
Features: A market square with a dry fountain, a Guild outpost, and Nessa's mill (the only working one for eight miles)
Significance: Where the Undergrowth first demonstrated its signature method — overwhelming numbers, zero violence, and Brandon's compulsion doing the real work — by having the entire town show up to a levy collection with empty hands

48.content:
Name: Thornwall
Summary: A walled river town roughly two days from the Dreadhold, home to the Guild's regional assessment office and the site of the confrontation with the Tailor
Features: Walls, water mills, a Guild depot with a slate roof by the river gate, and Master Halloway's independent side office on River Street
Significance: Where Brandon's true Guild card (SS Outcome Rating / F Combat Rating) was finalized, where four fake Chancellery inspectors tried to intercept and forge a replacement, and where the Tailor first revealed himself

49.content:
Name: Mosshaven
Summary: A village emptied by the Drinker's miasma in autumn, revived after the Drinker's defeat
Features: An aether-rich well, a crab apple tree blooming out of season, a nameless three-month-old grave the column stood over in silence, and — now — a silver sapling (the transformed Drinker) planted where it once burned a home
Future Plans: Being developed as a combined shrine (to Yminia), well (aether-rich water, prized by brewers and alchemists), waystation (on Mira's new river trade route), and memorial — 341 names, being read aloud one by one, village by village, by Edda
Status: Twelve returning families, home again as of Emberfall 18. Gruk has taken to watering the sapling and reciting names to it
Key Quotes:
- "This village is going to be the luckiest ground in three counties and none of them know it yet." — Edda

50.content:
Name: Dread Lords of Magisa (historical context)
Summary: "Dread Lord" is a real, rarely-claimed Guild class title with a specific and unflattering lineage, not a joke title Brandon invented. Per Gerald, who can name every one back to the Sundering: Malgrath the Unpleasant, Vess of the Weeping Tower, the Landlord King — "terrible people, every one, yet memorable. Memorable like a scar." Brandon's registration is the first since Vess of the Weeping Tower
Connection: Gerald's own inscriptions reference the Weeping Tower directly — his light "went dark once outside" it, meaning his runes failed near a genuine, powerful source of corruption, and relit only after that source was destroyed. He considers this proof that even the worst sources can be ended, which is part of why he takes Brandon's campaign against miasma seriously
Key Quotes:
- "I can name every Dread Lord back to the Sundering. Malgrath the Unpleasant. Vess of the Weeping Tower. The Landlord King. Terrible people, every one, yet memorable. Memorable like a scar." — Gerald

51.content:
Name: The Bigger Mystery (recursion hub — not lore on its own)
Purpose: A lightweight index entry. It carries no new information; it exists so a generic question like "what's actually going on here" or "is this all connected" pulls in the whole web of interlinked mysteries at once, via recursion, instead of requiring every individual thread to be named.
The Threads: Three tears in the weave connect Brandon, Clarissa, and the Drinker. The Tailor, a sash-man, personally caused two of those tears and suspects a third party interfered with his work afterward. The prophecy Yminia gave hints at a witness who saw Brandon cross between worlds. King Aurelian has holes where his memory should be, likely from the same pattern. And underneath the Feast of Saint Halvard sits a suspected Chancellery conspiracy, tied to a Lord Chancellor with a decades-long, unexplained account with Dowager Baroness Ophelia Wexley.
